dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to BLS Unit 2 for seizures at 2807 37th Street.
I'm Alex with rescue squad 2. What exactly happened today?
My son is 18 months old and just started seizing-eyes locked to the right with stiff limbs and lip-smacking.
Is he responsive to any stimuli?
No. He is unresponsive right now.
Is his gaze fixed or are his eyes open?
His gaze is fixed to the right.
Does he make any sounds during the seizure?
Occasionally, but nothing consistent. He's very unresponsive.
Any limb movement or response to touch during the episode?
Only stiffening and jerking. No response.
Let me check his airway. Is he breathing now?
He is breathing, but it's very shallow.
Airway is patent. I see no obstruction or need for intervention.
No visible obstructions. He's breathing shallow and very fast.
Respirations are shallow at a rapid rate. Let's count together. He's at about 30 per minute.
He doesn't show nasal flaring. Breathing is slightly labored but breathing is clearly ineffective right now.
Any signs of respiratory distress?
No flaring. Breathing is slightly labored, but I don't see accessory muscle use.
I'll check brachial pulse. Strong and rapid-about 150 per minute.
Skin is hot and dry. Cap refill is under 2 seconds.
SpO2 is 89-below normal. Let's start oxygen.
Starting nasal cannula oxygen at 4 LPM to improve his oxygen saturation.
SpO2 improved to 95 very quickly on the nasal cannula.
No DCAP-BTLS noted. Blood pressure is fluctuating at this time.
When did the seizure start?
As I was helping him to the crib, he curled his body into a stiff pose and began shaking.
Any known allergies or drug reactions?
No known allergies. He's only ever taken Albuterol-very rarely.
What medications is he currently taking?
Used Albuterol earlier today. Otherwise, no regular meds.
Has he had any past health issues or surgeries?
He was born at 35 weeks due to premature ROM but is otherwise healthy. No surgeries.
Last time he ate or drank?
It's been a few days of poor appetite, but he had fluids within the hour before the seizure.
What happened before the seizure today?
He's had a fever, cough, and malaise for three days. I gave him Tylenol at 2:00 PM.
His pulse is 150-very high and regular.
He has 30 breaths per minute and are shallow and regular.
SpO2 is at 95. That's a big improvement since we started oxygen.
Blood sugar is 127 mg/dL-normal range.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia with a strong, regular rhythm.
Blood pressure is difficult to obtain-fluctuating between 0/0 and 112.
Let's monitor BP continuously as we proceed.
Hot skin with no rashes or discoloration. Hot to the touch, capillary refill within 2 seconds.
Pupils are 2 mm, equal, and reactive.
Mild clear secretions noted from eyes and nose. Face is symmetrical. No signs of trauma.
Lungs are clear with mild expiratory wheezing bilaterally.
Brachial and popliteal pulses are strong and present; movement is present bilaterally post-seizure.
Let's apply the cardiac monitor and set up capnography now.
He's currently in his second seizure. Do you authorize administration of Midazolam?
Yes, please-do whatever you must.
Administering 2.0 mg of Midazolam IM to his left thigh now.
Placing the patient on a 4-lead ECG now as per protocol.
During the second seizure, his respiratory rate has dropped to 13 per minute and is very ineffective.
Blood pressure is reading 0/0 during the second seizure; this is commonly seen in status epilepticus.
Midazolam administered. Seizure has stopped. SpO2 is now 99 and HR has increased to 186.
Capnography shows CO2 at 41. Respiratory rate has risen to 41 per minute after medication.
He's crying, showing eye opening to pain, and is now more alert-GCS has improved to 9.
He is now responding to touch and is more alert. We are trending in a better direction.
His post-seizure state is flaccid. He's weak and shows minimal eye movement now.
Blood pressure reads 108/0. Still very low diastolic but heart rate remains elevated.
Based on the second generalized seizure and history of febrile seizures, we are following Seizure Protocol 9-12.
VCU Health was chosen for transport due to their pediatric and emergency expertise.
He's being placed on the stretcher and secured for transport with an ACR.
Are we moving him on his side for the ride?
Yes, he's being secured in a position that allows us to monitor him effectively.
Hospital, this is EMS 214. We have an 18-month-old male with ongoing seizure activity and febrile history en route to VCU.
Initial vitals: HR 150, RR 30, SpO2 89. Later vitals: HR 138, RR 35, SpO2 95, BP fluctuating.
Administered 2.0 mg of Midazolam IM at 20:09 for second generalized seizure. Vitals improved after intervention.
4-lead ECG and EtCO2 monitoring in place. He's breathing at 49 per minute with SpO2 at 99.
Presentation with febrile symptoms, poor appetite, and current oxygen administration. He's no longer seizing and responding to touch.
He is not showing pain and is alert enough to cry post-seizure. No signs of another seizure activity.
Reading blood pressure is now 108/0. We're stabilizing the situation and proceeding to the transport phase as planned.
Patient is flaccid post-event. We have him safely secured for transport.
